translated: Yeah, I put those feelings into it and the tears just started flowing naturally.
There were parts where I got choked up and couldn't get the words out,
but that's what acting is all about. If I'm crying as Ena, then the character on screen is crying too.
Since I was crying as Ena, she was crying with me.
Usually, the dialogue moves forward with a button press, but they adjusted to my timing.
They didn't cut the recording and cherished every moment until the button was pressed. I'm so grateful.
I've received so many comments saying this was a great story. And it wasn't just about how my acting was,
or how my voice sounded as Suzuki Minori.
Instead of focusing on me, everyone really took Ena's way of life to heart. Some felt encouraged to work hard too,
and long-time fans were just so happy for her.
Even though her journey is just beginning, I'm so glad people felt so much for Ena.
I poured my absolute all into Ena for this, so I'm really happy with how it turned out.
That's right.
Yeah.
Now, let's take a look at the illustrations.
By the way, I chose this one because it's the moment Ena finally sees a glimmer of hope after suffering for her art.
All right.
Let's spend about four minutes looking at the card illustrations together.
Before that, I have some comments from the illustration team about the costumes to share.
The theme of this story is Ena's struggle to get into art school, nearly giving up after falling short,
but ultimately deciding not to quit.
The motif is an art apron over a school uniform,
designed to capture Ena's look as a student preparing for entrance exams.
While she has other apron outfits, for this event, we gave Ena a patched-up apron.
This represents her persistence through repeated setbacks.
It's a unique arrangement for this specific story.
We kept strong uniform elements like the sailor collar and pleats,
but modified the shape to give it more of a costume feel.
That's the design philosophy behind it.
And here is the illustration.
The pre-trained version... everyone gave such great feedback on this one.
She looks like she's having fun painting.
Her hands are all messy.
It's so wonderful.
And here are the pre-trained and post-trained versions
featuring everyone who watched over and supported Ena.
Kanade is a creator herself,
so even though their genres are different, she understands Ena's feelings. And KAITO...
Yeah, KAITO gave Ena so many important words of advice.
They're all eating ice cream together. I named this one "Yogurt Ice Cream."
That's just because I personally love yogurt ice cream.
It's all about me, isn't it?
And here's Miku.
So cute.
Lovely, there are so many drawings.
She's eating ice cream too.
Look, ice cream!
So cute.
And finally, Mafu— wait, oops.
Ah, I can't get the long-press to work.
There we go.
She's holding the apron.
Yes, adorable.
Thank you so much.
So, you've seen the pre-trained and post-trained illustrations...
Oh, come on! How many times am I going to mess this up?
Anyway, those were the illustrations.
I have a few more minutes, so I'll talk a bit more about the story.
The previous story wasn't specifically about her art,
but the one before that...
where I sang the song "I am Rain."
During that story about Ena's painting—I don't like saying "drawings."
Anyway, during that story, I happened to visit an art museum.
I was able to act while reflecting on what it means to look at a painting.
It helped me understand Ena better.
This time, I was just walking down the street,
and I passed an actual art prep school, the kind Ena would attend.
I felt this intense tension just looking from the outside, and it reminded me how tough her world is before I went into recording.
I try to act every day in a way that conveys the reality of the place Ena is in.
Thank you.
All right.
So...
Um...
I still have a few dozen seconds left.
Some of you might have already seen the AfterLive by now.
It follows the content of Episode 8,
so you can see Ena's refreshed expression. Things will still be hard for her,
but I hope her resolve comes through in her expression and her singing.
I hope it's reaching all of you right now.
Yes.
